messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/
- dc: http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/
- dct: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/
- gn: http://www.geonames.org/ontology#
- hint: http://www.bigdata.com/queryHints#
- imdb: https://www.imdb.com/
- math: http://www.w3.org/2005/xpath-functions/math#
- ontolex: http://www.w3.org/ns/lemon/ontolex#
- owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#
- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/
- pqn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value-normalized/
- pqv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value/
- pr: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/
- prn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value-normalized/
- prov: http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#
- prv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value/
- ps: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/
- psn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value-normalized/
- psv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value/
- qb: http://purl.org/linked-data/cube#
- qfn: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/function#
- ql: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/builtin-functions/
- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/
- s: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/statement/
- schema: http://schema.org/
- skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#
- v: http://www.wikidata.org/value/
- void: http://rdfs.org/ns/void#
- wd: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/
- wdno: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/novalue/
- wdt: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/
- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#
- xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.
List all Roman Emperors along with their place of birth.

content.message:
To list all Roman Emperors along with their place of birth, I need to:

1. Identify entities that represent Roman Emperors in Wikidata.
2. Retrieve the property that links an emperor to their place of birth.
3. Fetch the place of birth for each identified Roman Emperor.

Step 1: Search for Roman Emperors in Wikidata.
I will search for entities related to "Roman Emperor" to identify the relevant entities.

Step 2: Once I have the list of Roman Emperors, I will retrieve their place of birth using the relevant property (likely a property like "place of birth" in Wikidata).

Step 3: Construct a SPARQL query that retrieves both the emperor and their place of birth.

Let's start by searching for entities related to "Roman Emperor" in Wikidata.

tool_calls.name: search_entity
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 entity alternatives:
1. Roman emperor (wd:Q842606), also known as "Cesar", "Emperor of Rome":
  - is instance of historical position
  - is subclass of emperor
  - ruler of the Roman Empire during the imperial period
2. Roman emperor (wd:Q10530454), also known as "Emperor of Rome":
  - is instance of Wikimedia disambiguation page
  - Wikimedia disambiguation page
3. Roman Emperor (wd:Q7361955):
  - 19th-century English sailing ship
  - is instance of watercraft
4. Roman Emperor (wd:Q7361952):
  - is instance of Wikimedia list article
  - Wikimedia list article
5. Roman Emperor (wd:Q7361953):
  - is instance of Wikimedia list article
  - wikimedia list article
6. Roman Emperor (wd:Q7361954):
  - is instance of Wikimedia list article
  - wikimedia list article
7. Roman emperor (wd:L275709-S1)
8. Roman emperor (wd:L453060-S2)
9. Roman emperor (wd:L495679-S1)
10. Holy Roman Emperor (wd:Q181765), also known as "Imperator Romanus Sacer", "Kaiser":
  - emperor of the Holy Roman Empire
  - is instance of elective office
  - is instance of head of state
  - is instance of historical position
  - is instance of noble title
  - is subclass of emperor
  - is subclass of emperor of the Occident

content.message:
The search result indicates that "Roman emperor" as an entity is primarily associated with the historical position or a disambiguation page. The most relevant result appears to be the entity "Roman emperor" (wd:Q842606), which is described as a historical position and a subclass of "emperor." 

Next, I will search for properties related to "place of birth" to determine the correct property identifier used in Wikidata to represent a person's place of birth.

I will now search for properties related to "place of birth."

tool_calls.name: search_property
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 property alternatives:
1. place of birth (wd:P19 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "birth city", "birth location", "birth place", "birthplace", "born", etc.:
  - most specific known birth location of a person, animal or fictional character
2. residence (wd:P551 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "hometown"), also known as "address", "has resided in", "home city", "home town", "hometown", etc.:
  - the place where the person is or has been, resident
3. category for people born here (wd:P1464 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "category for place of birth"), also known as "category for place of birth", "category of people born here":
  - category item that groups people born in this place
4. location of formation (wd:P740 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "place of formation"), also known as "comes from", "formation location", "formation place", "formed in", "founded in", etc.:
  - location where a group or organization was formed
5. location of creation (wd:P1071 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "place of origin"), also known as "assembly location", "built at", "built in", "composed at", "created at", etc.:
  - place where the item was conceived or made; where applicable, location of final assembly
6. country of origin (wd:P495 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "place of origin"), also known as "comes from", "CoO", "country", "homeland", "originates from", etc.:
  - country of origin of this item (creative work, food, phrase, product, etc.)
7. location (wd:P276 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "place"), also known as "based in", "city", "exact location", "from", "held by", etc.:
  - location of the object, structure or event; use P131 to ... entities; use P7153 for locations associated with the object
8. ranking (wd:P1352 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "place"), also known as "peak position", "place", "placing", "position", "rank":
  - subject's ordinal position as qualitatively evaluated relative to other members of a group
9. valid in place (wd:P3005 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "place"), also known as "applies to country", "applies to location", "applies to nonjurisdictional location", "applies to nonjurisdictional place", "applies to place", etc.:
  - place where a statement is valid
10. applies to jurisdiction (wd:P1001 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "of place"), also known as "applicable area", "applicable country", "applicable geographic place", "applicable jurisdiction", "applicable location", etc.:
  - the item (institution, law, public office, public register, ... jurisdiction: a country, state, municipality, etc)

content.message:
The property "place of birth" is clearly identified as `wdt:P19` in Wikidata. This property is associated with the label "place of birth" and is commonly used to denote the birth location of a person.

Now, I need to identify Roman Emperors as subjects and retrieve their `P19` (place of birth) property values. 

To achieve this, I will:
1. Search for entities related to Roman Emperors, specifically focusing on historical individuals (not just the position or disambiguation page).
2. Use the entity with "Roman Emperor" and filter to individuals who are actual historical figures (not abstract positions).
3. Query Wikidata for all known Roman Emperors and their place of birth using the property `wdt:P19`.

I will first refine the search to identify actual individuals who were Roman Emperors by searching for "Roman Emperor" with constraints to ensure we are looking for people and not just positions or disambiguation pages.

I will search for entities with the label "Roman Emperor" and check if any specific individuals are identified (e.g., "Augustus," "Trajan," etc.).

tool_calls.name: search_entity
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 entity alternatives:
1. Augustus (wd:Q1405), also known as "Augustus Caesar", "Caesar Augustus", "Caesar Octavianus", "Caius Julius Cæsar Octavianus", "Caius Octavius", etc.:

  - has occupation military personnel
  - has occupation politician
  - has occupation ruler
  - is instance of human
2. Augustus (wd:Q211804), also known as "Avgvstvs":
  - imperial rank, honorific and title
  - is instance of agnomen
  - is instance of cognomen
  - is instance of honorific
  - is instance of noble title
  - is instance of title of honor
3. Augustus (wd:Q346610):
  - is instance of Wikimedia disambiguation page
  - Wikimedia disambiguation page
4. Imperium: Augustus (wd:Q459427, matched via "Augustus"), also known as "Augustus":
  - 2003 film by Roger Young
  - is instance of film
5. Augustus (wd:Q4821361):
  - 1972 novel by John Edward Williams
  - is instance of written work
6. Augustus (wd:Q770041), also known as "Augustus (first name)", "Augustus (given name)":
  - is instance of male given name
  - male given name
7. Rise of Augustus (wd:Q16054479, matched via "Augustus"), also known as "Augustus":
  - 2013 board game
  - is instance of board game
8. Augulus (wd:Q108525857, matched via "Augustus"), also known as "Augurius", "Augustus", "Aule", "Ouil", "Saint Augulus":
  - 3rd or 4th century bishop and martyr in a town called Augusta in Britain, or perhaps in Normandy
  - is instance of human
9. Augustus (wd:Q113574811):
  - is instance of name
  - name
10. Augustus (wd:Q17150377):
  - 1986 novel by Allan Massie
  - is instance of literary work

content.message:
The search result clearly shows that `wd:Q1405` is the entity for Augustus, the first Roman emperor. This confirms that we can identify Roman Emperors by searching for specific names or their known titles.

To systematically find all known Roman Emperors, I will now search for entities related to "Roman Emperor" and filter to individuals who are instances of humans (not titles, films, or disambiguation pages). 

I will conduct a search for "Roman Emperor" with the constraint that the subject is an instance of a human.
<tool_call>
{"name": "search_with_constraints", "arguments": {"kg": "wikidata", "index": "entity", "position": "subject", "query": "Roman Emperor", "constraints": {"wdt:P31": "wd:Q5"}}
</tool_call>
